title: Turbine Cycle Trade Study
description: Compare turbojet, turbofan, turboprop, or hybrid concepts against a chosen mission and explain which propulsion architecture is the best fit.
whyItMatters: Propulsion choices are never only about thrust. They shape efficiency, noise, maintenance, mission speed, and the business case for the aircraft.

steps.title: Choose the mission
steps.description: Set the range, cruise speed, altitude, noise environment, and payload expectations that the propulsion system must satisfy.
steps.tips: A propulsion answer without a mission is just a preference.

steps.title: Define candidate cycles
steps.description: Pick two or three propulsion architectures and summarise their typical strengths and weaknesses.
steps.tips: Keep the comparison fair by using realistic classes, not idealised best cases.

steps.title: Estimate performance
steps.description: Compare thrust or power suitability, fuel burn trend, mass impact, and likely integration complexity.
steps.tips: Simple first-order calculations are enough if the logic is clear.

steps.title: Check non-performance factors
steps.description: Add maintenance, operating cost, noise, and certification implications to the trade.
steps.tips: The most efficient engine is not always the best programme choice.

steps.title: Make the recommendation
steps.description: Write a short decision memo naming the preferred cycle and the key assumptions that support it.
steps.tips: State what would need to change before you would pick the second-best option.

rubric.description: The propulsion choice is clearly tied to the mission demands.
Performance, integration, and operational factors are balanced rather than cherry-picked.
Cycle-level arguments are correct and easy to follow.
The recommendation is decisive but honest about uncertainty.
